***	In keeping with paragraph 10 of General Assembly resolution 77/267, the part consisting of the programme plan and programme performance information (part II) is submitted through the Committee for Programme and Coordination for the consideration of the Assembly.

text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

29C.1	The Office of Information and Communications Technology is responsible for the direction, strategy and delivery of information and communications technology (ICT) in the United Nations. The mandate derives from relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolutions 63/262 and 72/266 B.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

29C.2	In 2027, the Office will continue to implement its programme of work in support of the achievement of the ICT strategic outcomes, ensuring an environment in which ICT enables coherent, efficient and integrated mandate implementation in the Organization and protects the Organization from rapidly escalating threats to ICT security.

29C.3	The Office will remain committed to providing resilient and secure global network and ICT systems, platforms and applications that enable collaboration and communication across the Secretariat and throughout the wider United Nations common system.

29C.4	The transformative potential of technology, including artificial intelligence and cloud-based platforms, enables United Nations personnel to collaborate and connect seamlessly. The Office will continue its strategic shift of leveraging hybrid cloud-based services and strengthen ICT security while maintaining enterprise on-premise solutions for legitimate business cases, such as compliance with specific resolutions and mandates. This hybrid multi-cloud approach strengthens operational resilience, supports business continuity and disaster recovery, mitigates single-vendor dependency and lock-in risks, and enables the Organization to balance central control with operational flexibility.

29C.5	In the context of ICT security, challenges are expected to continue to grow in scale, pervasiveness and complexity. The main risks foreseen for 2027 include threats amplified by artificial intelligence and increased vulnerabilities due to ageing equipment and infrastructure. In response, the Office will reinforce centralized ICT security capacity and enhance the use of shared services of proactive threat-hunting and vulnerability management capabilities. The centralization of ICT security services will strengthen internal capacity, while a clear focus is kept on United Nations global operations. Other key initiatives are also under way, including continuous improvements in networks, which are instrumental in containing threats and limiting the impact of successful attacks; the upgrade of various solutions to detect and prevent security issues and to protect end-user devices, digital identities and cloud-based services; and increased interactive security awareness campaigns.

29C.6	The Office will continue to support efforts to digitally transform the Organization into one that more effectively uses data and information to support decision-making and inform strategic direction. This work will be aligned with the ICT strategy (see A/77/489 and A/79/339) and the Data Strategy of the Secretary-General for Action by Everyone, Everywhere. The effective use of data across the Secretariat requires cross-cutting efforts. The Office will establish services and technology capacities that support the management and use of data. The Office will continue to leverage technology enablers that support the exchange, reconciliation, storage and governance of data. Analytics and data solutions will also continue to be implemented, and associated standards and architecture will be formulated to achieve a consistent approach to data. Further, the Office plans to support policy formulation and the governance of information management, including the use and adoption of artificial intelligence.

29C.7	In support of innovation, the Office will continue to provide access to technologies that can support a wide range of needs across the United Nations. Frontier technologies, in particular conversational artificial intelligence deployed in the productivity tools, will support the work of the Organization and more intuitive and efficient mandate delivery globally.

29C.8	The Office will further advance its work in digital transformation, strengthening business relationships between the Office’s service providers and United Nations Secretariat entities by facilitating clear communication, managing demands effectively and ensuring effective problem resolution. The Office will also continue to serve as a centralized function for global enterprise ICT project governance through the enterprise programme and portfolio management team, ensuring that projects comply with the project management methodology, achieve desired outcomes and are aligned with the approved objectives. It will also ensure that overall Secretariat ICT budget proposals are technically reviewed by the Office, leading to improved accountability of the Secretariat to Member States regarding the efficient use of ICT resources. The internal consolidation of the business relationship management and the enterprise programme and portfolio management functions will strengthen the Office’s oversight role in implementing the Organization’s ICT strategy, provide consistent and accountable mechanisms for engagement between entities and the Office of Information and Communications Technology, ensure effective project management and enhance Secretariat-wide coherence, transparency and prioritization in the planning, governance and execution of ICT initiatives.

29C.9	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Office will continue to collaborate with agencies, funds and programmes to agree on minimum standards of ICT security and promote the establishment of communities of practice in key ICT areas to discuss opportunities and challenges at the inter-agency level. This will be done through the United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination Digital and Technology Network, which is co-chaired by the Assistant Secretary-General/Chief Information Technology Officer and serves as a mechanism to collaborate on, harmonize and explore possibilities for better use of shared ICT services. The Office will also continue to ensure access to Secretariat platforms for personnel from United Nations agencies, funds and programmes to facilitate collaboration and will strategize on the mitigation of cost increases and minimizing duplication across the United Nations common system to support coherence in mandate delivery.

29C.10	In line with the recent guidance provided by Member States, the Office will establish functional reporting between information and communications technology chiefs of United Nations entities and the Office, maintaining their existing administrative reporting lines while enhancing functional reporting and ensuring coherence, coordination and accountability across the ICT function without creating parallel or isolated structures. This functional reporting will strengthen enterprise-wide alignment and consistency in ICT governance and delivery, complement established reporting mechanisms and support implementation of the Organization’s ICT strategic direction, as reflected in the ICT strategy.

29C.11	The Office integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. The Office will continue to promote women in technology within the Secretariat and to highlight the contributions of women in ICT.

29C.12	With regard to external factors, the Office considered applicable risks identified in its risk register. The proposed programme plan for 2027 is based on the planning assumption that there will be adequate voluntary contributions through effective partnering to complement regular budget resources to carry out the activities of the Office.

29C.13	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Office will strengthen digital accessibility to Secretariat digital platforms by coordinating outreach and awareness, providing technical capacity to conduct digital accessibility assessments and monitoring progress towards compliance with established standards. The Office will also improve physical accessibility at Headquarters by providing an audio-based navigation solution to help persons with visual impairments navigate designated indoor and outdoor areas.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

29C.14	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. For example, adjustments aimed at reducing the fragmentation of ICT infrastructure are presented under subprogramme 2, result 3; and increased oversight, coherence, accountability and more efficient and effective use of ICT resources will be enabled by the functional ICT reporting between entities and the Office of Information and Communications Technology.

29C.15	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning future proposals to consolidate Secretariat ICT enterprise-level capacities and functions to reduce fragmentation and help ensure that enterprise technology initiatives operate under a single, accountable governance framework.
Legislative mandates
29C.16	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the programme.
General Assembly resolutions

table_content.text: Information and communications technology, enterprise resource planning, and security, disaster recovery and business continuity

children.text: Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Strategy and technology innovation
Objective
29C.18	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to enhance an innovative, secure, coherent and unified digital space for the United Nations.
Strategy

29C.19	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will continue to focus on policies and governance, the development of technology strategies and the establishment of technical procedures and guidelines. It will also continue to focus on managing ICT demand and implementing and monitoring ICT project management frameworks, as well as on a review of standards and architecture. Specifically, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Provide advice covering data, analytics, artificial intelligence, innovation and information management;

(b)	Develop technology standards and road maps to envisage and define how United Nations technologies should be implemented in support of the core mandates of the United Nations (see General Assembly resolution 69/262), taking into account organizational needs, as well as strategic direction and innovation;

(c)	Work closely with United Nations clients to identify their key data, information and technology requirements or challenges and, in close collaboration with internal and external partners, design innovative, integrated and security-resilient technology solutions, leveraging artificial intelligence, and facilitate an enabling environment to meet clients’ needs;

(d)	Support and coordinate with United Nations entities in assessing and reducing risks and threats to data, operations and the safety of personnel and ICT assets, while ensuring compliance, through the provision of specific ICT security guidance, the performance of system security reviews and the conduct of ICT security awareness training;
(e)	Reinforce the centralized capacity for ICT security in the Enterprise Data Centre in Valencia, Spain;
(f)	Promote ICT security awareness and the use of advanced security features, and support security measures through strengthening vulnerability, resilience and threat management;

(g)	Promote the effective use of data, facilitate access to data across the Organization by leveraging modern technology, strengthen data literacy by co-leading the implementation of the Data Strategy of the Secretary-General and govern information and data management.
29C.20	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Better awareness of and access to innovation techniques and new technologies, and increased capacity for Secretariat entities to deliver on mandates;

(b)	Strengthened operational resilience of the Organization, better end-user awareness of ICT security threats and reduced exposure of the digital data and infrastructure of the United Nations to various security threats;

(c)	Reinforced and centralized ICT security, with core capacity for proactive vulnerability management and threat and incident management, that reduces the risk of adverse impacts on mandate implementation while ensuring global Secretariat coordination and support on ICT security matters, including compliance;
(d)	Integrated, secure and innovative use of information and data by United Nations entities;

(e)	A coherent, and non-duplicative, ICT landscape in the Secretariat that enables efficient and effective implementation of mandates with enhanced ICT demand management and better project and portfolio management;
(f)	Improved responses to rapidly evolving security risks and a reduced likelihood of security incidents.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved information and security technology security awareness to safeguard the Secretariat

29C.21	As the United Nations becomes increasingly digitally interconnected, ICT security awareness remains essential to enabling personnel to respond effectively to continuously evolving threats, safeguarding ICT assets, including data, and ensuring business continuity. The subprogramme promotes awareness through the provision of specialized ICT security awareness sessions conducted during observations of ICT Security Awareness Month. Since 2019, these sessions have been offered to Secretariat personnel, covering key topics such as social engineering, digital hygiene practices, the backing up of data and systems, the use of strong passwords and multifactor authentication, and emerging artificial intelligence-enabled security threats. The subprogramme delivered four new virtual sessions and one in-person session. In addition, more than 30 new ICT security resources containing guidance for United Nations entities were developed and distributed for local use by such entities on specific topics to help raise awareness and develop practical skills. Over time, sustained awareness contributes to fostering the development of a stronger security culture in the Organization, reduces the likelihood and impact of ICT security incidents and supports the reliable delivery of the Organization’s mandates.
29C.22	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29C.2).
Table 29C.2
Performance measure

children.text: Result 3: enable Secretariat entities to apply the information and communications technology strategy effectively for improved mandate delivery – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29C.27	The subprogramme led the development of the ICT strategy and its refined elements in collaboration with Secretariat entities in 2022, 2023 and 2024, establishing a coherent and effective direction for ICT in the Secretariat for the period 2025–2029. The General Assembly requested an annual implementation report, with the first to be presented at its eightieth session.
Lessons learned and planned change

29C.28	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the implementation of the ICT strategy requires detailed monitoring and reporting supported by a clear implementation road map with defined timelines, milestones, resource allocation and key performance indicators. This will help ensure measurable benefits such as enhanced service delivery, risk reduction and adherence to established ICT governance processes. In applying the lesson learned from previous initiatives, the subprogramme will work closely with United Nations entities to measure, evaluate and report on progress to ensure accountability, early issue identification and sustained realization of strategic value.
29C.29	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29C.5).

children.text: Deliverables
29C.30	Table 29C.6 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29C.6
Subprogramme 1: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Information and communications technology: ICT and information management policies and standards; enterprise architecture, ICT risks and compliance oversight mechanism; ICT project and portfolio management governance, framework, methodology and compliance oversight; services to Secretariat entities enabled by generative artificial intelligence; ICT security awareness campaigns; expert advice and training to Secretariat entities on information management; disaster recovery plans for critical systems; and information security framework across Secretariat entities, comprising a framework document on information security; security assessment of systems and entities; Women in Tech campaigns, Digital Accessibility and Inclusion campaign, and Digital Unite campaign highlighting ICT innovations; business relationships and demand coordination across 99 Secretariat entities; ICT budget reviews for 50 regular budget entities, 19 special political missions and 12 peacekeeping missions; and contract management of 107 enterprise-level ICT contracts.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Operations support
Objective

29C.31	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure that the Secretariat benefits from resilient, secure and effective ICT services that enable consistent, reliable and modern digital operations across the Organization.
Strategy

29C.32	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will prioritize enterprise ICT infrastructure consolidation, the modernization of enterprise systems and the optimization of ICT services, to enable the Secretariat and Member States to effectively deliver on the Organization’s work. Specifically, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Deliver personal productivity support services that enable United Nations personnel to collaborate, communicate and work efficiently in one integrated digital environment;

(b)	Enable enterprise systems and infrastructure to support the Secretariat in operationalizing data, innovation and digital transformation through artificial intelligence-driven systems and self-service analytics;
(c)	Modernize identity, access and endpoint management through cloud-based authentication and standardized workstation management;
(d)	Promote and extend the utilization of standardized geographic information systems services;
(e)	Implement a hybrid multi-cloud strategy to achieve efficiencies through the migration of workloads into enterprise data centres or approved public cloud service providers;
(f)	Enhance the enterprise service desk to deliver comprehensive support for global ICT services and enterprise applications such as Umoja, Inspira and Unite Mail;
(g)	Provide broadcast, audiovisual and conferencing technologies to support intergovernmental meetings and the work of Secretariat entities.
29C.33	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved implementation of mandates by Secretariat entities using modern, resilient and secure global ICT infrastructure and services;

(b) 	Strengthened security and improved resilience, compliance and disaster recovery across duty stations while reducing duplication through the increased use of shared enterprise ICT services across the Secretariat;
(c)	A modern and secure technology environment that provides simplified access to the Organization’s data and serves as the foundation for the Data Strategy of the Secretary-General;
(d)	Timely resolution of incidents and service requests, with users expressing increased satisfaction with ICT enterprise services;

(e)	Improved access to intergovernmental meetings, including in respect of remote participation, the access of Secretariat services to verbatim report-writing and translation, and the access of other audiences to interpretation, broadcasting and webcasting capabilities.
Programme performance in 2025
More reliable and stable connectivity for delegates and staff due to improved Wi-Fi at Headquarters

29C.34	The subprogramme addressed the critical need for reliable and secure wireless connectivity at Headquarters to support intergovernmental processes, daily Secretariat operations and the increasing reliance on digital collaboration tools by delegates, staff and other personnel. The subprogramme implemented targeted upgrades to the wireless infrastructure at Headquarters, focusing on improving coverage, capacity and service delivery in the intergovernmental conference rooms, meeting areas and high-traffic workspaces in the General Assembly Building, Conference Building and Secretariat Building. These efforts included the phased replacement and optimization of wireless access points, alignment with enterprise network standards and enhanced performance monitoring. As a result, these efforts contributed to improved user experience, reduced disruptions during meetings and conferences, and strengthened the overall resilience of ICT services supporting mandate delivery.
29C.35	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29C.I).
Figure 29C.I
Performance measure: number of Wi-Fi incidents
(Unit of measurement)
Planned results for 2027

29C.37	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 29C.7).
Table 29C.7
Performance measure

children.text: Result 2: reduced risks through cloud-based authentication for secure access – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29C.38	The subprogramme’s work contributed to reduced ICT security risks and increased operational resilience by migrating 6,200 user workstations to the cloud, which exceeded the target of 4,500 workstations.
29C.39	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29C.II).
Figure 29C.II
Performance measure: number of user workstations at Headquarters that have been migrated to cloud-based authentication (cumulative)
Result 3: reduced fragmentation and strengthened information and communications technology resilience, security and standardization of data centre infrastructure – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29C.40	The consolidation of ICT infrastructure and hosting environments is critical to improving operational resilience, reducing duplication and containing operating costs across the Secretariat. Fragmented hosted environments increase complexity, elevate ICT security risks and limit the Organization’s ability to achieve economies of scale and standardized service delivery. The subprogramme has advanced the implementation of the hybrid multi-cloud strategy, including the migration of selected workloads from locally managed infrastructure to enterprise data centres, and approved cloud platforms. These efforts contributed to improved service reliability, strengthened disaster recovery arrangements, ensured more efficient use of shared enterprise infrastructure and allowed entities to reduce reliance on aged, underutilized and duplicative hosting environments.
Lessons learned and planned change

29C.41	The lesson for the subprogramme was that infrastructure consolidation delivers the greatest impact when supported by clear enterprise governance and early engagement with Secretariat entities to align operational goals, creating greater coherence in ICT across the Organization. Experience also confirms that phased migration and improved visibility of data centres and server rooms are critical to managing risk and minimizing disruption during consolidation activities. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen enterprise-level planning and coordination for data centre consolidation, which will be managed through a structured project and working closely with the entities to prioritize workload transitions to enterprise data centres or approved cloud platforms, supporting a more systematic, predictable and scalable approach to consolidation and enabling reduced fragmentation and operating costs.
29C.42	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29C.III).
Figure 29C.III
Performance measure: number of entity-managed data centres or server rooms

children.text: a	The scope of services is limited to New York.
Subprogramme 3 – Enterprise solutions
Objective
29C.44	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the effective and efficient implementation of mandates by Secretariat entities.
Strategy
29C.45	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Continue to develop bespoke software solutions that support mandate delivery and the Sustainable Development Goals by partnering with Secretariat entities and leveraging emerging technologies to meet evolving challenges and mandates;
(b)	Actively support the digital transformation of the Secretariat by designing process automation and knowledge management solutions for departments and offices;
(c)	Implement the Data Strategy of the Secretary-General by delivering technical solutions for data exchange between systems through an enterprise platform;
(d)	Enhance digital transformation by integrating artificial intelligence into ICT solutions;

(e)	Enhance identity management by leveraging cloud technology, upgrade service management solutions, enhance talent management, manage the United Nations safety and security information systems, and design and operate the enterprise web content management platform.
29C.46	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Accelerated progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals;
(b)	Increased efficiency of Secretariat entities in implementing their mandates;
(c)	Expanded data-sharing between and among the Organization’s stakeholders, coordination and cooperation within and across the Secretariat and the broader United Nations system;
(d)	Hiring managers and other human resources staff benefit from advanced tools that ensure compliance with the new staff selection policy;
(e)	Increased availability of information for professional security officers to perform their duties of protecting United Nations personnel across the United Nations system;

(f)	Increased accessibility to public information websites, as well as their compliance with the Secretariat’s guidance on United Nations website publishing, technology, security, accessibility, branding and multilingualism.
Programme performance in 2025
Increased availability of websites and applications by migrating away from the primary technology centre of New York

29C.47	The primary technology centre in New York has been reducing its footprint after two serious incidents, involving flooding and a power cut, in recent years. Alternative hosting is being offered to avoid similar disruptions in the future. The subprogramme assisted departments and offices with planning, coordinating and migrating the hosting of websites and applications away from the centre.
29C.48	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29C.9).

children.text: Result 3: enhanced standards, security, multilingualism and accessibility of United Nations public information websites – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29C.53	The Enterprise Content Management platform provides public website authoring entities in the Organization with the ability to easily deploy new websites that are compliant in the areas of information security, usability, United Nations branding, multilingualism and accessibility for persons with disabilities, while leveraging economies of scale for platform support and operations. The subprogramme has continuously onboarded existing websites on the platform since it became operational, and upgraded its underlying technology. It will continue to do so in the coming years, with the aim of migrating all websites managed by the Office of Information and Communications Technology to the platform.
Lessons learned and planned change

29C.54	The lesson for the subprogramme was the importance of minimizing the effort of onboarding websites on the Enterprise Content Management platform from a technology point of view and focusing on the contents. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will design automation scripts, where possible, to make the technical migration of legacy websites effortless so that departments and offices can dedicate more time to updating the contents, if required.
29C.55	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29C.IV).
Figure 29C.IV
Performance measure: percentage of websites managed by the Office of Information and Communications Technology with enhanced accessibility and compliant with standards, security and multilingualism
Deliverables
29C.56	Table 29C.12 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29C.12
Subprogramme 3: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Information and communications technology: content management solutions for the Secretariat; enterprise website content management platform, including security, standards, accessibility and multilingualism compliance, used by Secretariat entities, field missions and permanent missions; talent management solutions; uniformed capabilities management solution compliant with the 2026 recommendations of the Working Group on Contingent-Owned Equipment; service management solutions for approximately 38,000 users; safety and security solutions for 140,600 users; digital ecosystem dealing with misconduct, sexual exploitation and abuse; application programme interface platform to foster process automation and system interoperability; data analytics tools for strategic planning; custom solutions to support mandate delivery of entities; and hypercare after the deployment of enterprise solutions to the new United Nations Support Office in Haiti.
